title: Lesson 19 — The Feud

words.example: His boorish behavior at dinner embarrassed everyone.
It seemed churlish to refuse such a generous gift.
A surly doorman who never once smiled.

words.definition: bad-tempered, argumentative, and difficult to deal with
words.example: A cantankerous old neighbor who sued everyone in sight.

words.definition: having or showing a tendency to be easily angered
words.example: An irascible chef who threw pans when a dish came back.

words.definition: eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant
words.example: A truculent reply that practically invited a brawl.
A belligerent drunk looking for someone to fight.

words.definition: eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight
words.example: A pugnacious debater who attacked every single point.

words.definition: causing or likely to cause an argument; controversial
words.example: A contentious topic that split the room in two.

words.definition: angry and bitter, especially of speech or a dispute
words.example: An acrimonious divorce dragged through the courts for years.
Years of rancor slowly poisoned the whole family.

words.definition: filled with bitter criticism or malice
words.example: A vitriolic review that felt weirdly personal.

story: The feud between the two households kept the whole street on edge. Old Harker was [[boorish|boorish]] and [[churlish|churlish]], perpetually [[surly|surly]], while his rival across the fence was [[cantankerous|cantankerous]] and [[irascible|irascible]], flaring up at nothing. Their sons were worse — [[truculent|truculent]], [[belligerent|belligerent]], and openly [[pugnacious|pugnacious]]. Every fence line became a [[contentious|contentious]] border and every greeting an [[acrimonious|acrimonious]] exchange. Decades of [[rancor|rancor]] hardened into habit, and the notes the two families left each other grew steadily more [[vitriolic|vitriolic]].
